text:
BOTTOM LINE — Risk-on, semis and cyclicals led. SOXX gained 1.59% while SPY added 0.54% and IWM rose 0.87%, with investors rotating into economically sensitive groups as the SpaceX IPO chatter and U.S.-Iran peace-deal reports supported a cautiously pro-growth tape.[5] Elevated breadth in small caps versus large (STYLE: SMALL > LARGE by 0.38pp) and strength in value factors alongside tech leadership point to positioning shifting toward a broader risk-on stance rather than pure mega-cap AI concentration.[5]

INDICES — VIX closed at 17.7, slightly above its 20-day moving average of 17.5, signaling modestly elevated volatility relative to recent history.[5] Daily performance ranked: SOXX +1.59% > IJR +0.97% > RSP +0.91% > IWM +0.87% > SLV +0.77% > DIA +0.73% > IJH +0.72% > QQQ +0.59% > SPY +0.54% > GLD +0.06%, with small beating large by 0.38pp on the STYLE line.[5] The most notable cross-asset move was USO dropping 2.64% as crude extended a 20-day slide of 12.29%, underscoring ongoing energy weakness.[5]

VOLUME — SPY traded at a volume ratio of 1.0x by construction of the index basket, while QQQ saw 1.4x average volume, indicating elevated attention to tech versus baseline.[5] DIA at 1.9x and IWM at 1.5x with prices higher signal accumulation in cyclicals and small caps, while sub-1.0x volume in SOXX, IJH, and IJR suggests today’s semiconductor and broader small-cap strength came without aggressive chase flows.[5]

SECTORS & ROTATION — XLB Materials +1.87%, XLF Financials +1.37%, XLU Utilities +1.09%, XLRE Real Estate +0.98%, XLK Technology +0.87%, XLE Energy +0.75%, XLP Staples +0.65%, XLI Industrials +0.59%, XLY Discretionary +0.26%, XLV Healthcare -0.18%, XLC Communications -0.42%.[5] Top industries were SILJ +3.23% and WGMI +3.21%, while ITA -0.95% and MSOS -4.36% lagged the most.[5] Money rotated into materials, financials, and rate-sensitive defensives (utilities, REITs) while leaking out of communication services and cannabis, consistent with a pro-cyclical but still yield-aware bias.[5] With XLK positive but not dominant and broad cyclical participation, the rotation-state label is broadening participation.[5]

POSITIONING — SPY’s put-call volume ratio at 0.80 indicates mild call bias and limited downside protection demand, while QQQ’s 1.22 print shows elevated put buying and more explicit hedging in tech.[5] Positive skew in both SPY (+8.1%) and QQQ (+16.5%) confirms put premia and a volatility surface consistent with downside fear even as spot trades above their respective gamma flip levels of 748.0 for SPY and 730.0 for QQQ, implying dealer positioning should dampen rather than amplify near-term moves.[5]

CROSS-ASSET — Oil’s 2.64% decline in USO alongside gains in materials (XLB +1.87%) and precious-metal miners (GDX +2.97%, SILJ +3.23%) highlights a divergence where industrial metals and miners trade more off growth and SpaceX-driven sentiment than off the broader commodity complex.[5] Flat UUP on the day with GLD only +0.06% despite equity strength reinforces that today’s risk-on move was equity- and theme-led rather than driven by macro FX or real-yield shifts.[5]

SIGNALS & WATCH — Fear & Greed at 70.6 (Greed) situates sentiment in optimistic territory, while among single names ALB surged 7.72% on strong lithium demand commentary, KLAC collapsed 89.45% on 9.1x volume, and ADBE dropped 6.76% on 4.2x volume within software.[5] In 20-day momentum terms, XLV leads sectors at +6.00% while XLC lags at -3.82%, and across baskets SOXX at +12.49% vs SLV at -18.83% mark the extremes.[5] KLAC’s vol spike with price down is textbook distribution in a core semi equipment bellwether, while ADBE’s high-volume selloff flags potential de-rating pressure inside high-multiple software.[5] Watch MCHI (+2.71%) for follow-through on 2.4x volume and EM beta, IYZ (+1.27%) for confirmation of its 3.4x volume breakout in telecoms, and ALB (+7.72%) as a test of whether lithium’s demand narrative can sustain the materials bid beyond today’s headline-driven pop.[5]
